# Project Overview {.unnumbered}



## Purpose {.unnumbered}
The purpose of the Watershed Connectivity Restoration Plan (WCRP) is to systematically identify and assess potential barriers to Atlantic salmon migration in the Cheticamp River Watershed. By conducting both desktop and field assessments, the project aims to evaluate the extent of migration barriers caused by manmade structures and identify opportunities for increasing connectivity for salmonid species. This assessment will guide the prioritization of barriers for remediation, ensuring that the most impactful sites are addressed first to enhance Atlantic salmon habitat and improve migration conditions. 

## Current Connectivity Status {.unnumbered}
In the Cheticamp River watershed, # structures potentially disconnect Atlantic Salmon habitat. Of these, # are identified as barriers in need of rehabilitation (priority barriers) and # require further field assessment. 
 


## Habitat Accumulation Curve {.unnumbered}
```{r echo = FALSE}
#| results: hide
#| message: false
#| warning: false

source("content/Rscripts/create_HAC.R")
plot_cumulative_avg_gain("content/data/combined_tracking_crossings_LNIC.csv")

```
![Map of focal species habitat and structures that are confirmed or potential barriers to fish passage in the Cheticamp River watershed as of February 27th, 2026. Structure data were obtained from the Canadian Aquatic Barriers Database (aquaticbarriers.ca). The accessibility model represents areas of the watershed that focal species could access naturally in the absence of anthropogenic barriers. The habitat model represents the subset of accessible areas that may be used by focal species for key habitat function(s). Available local knowledge and data were incorporated and overrule habitat model results. Thick light-blue lines represent habitat that is currently connected, whereas thick red lines represent habitat considered to be disconnected because it is upstream of confirmed barriers or data-deficient structures. The y-axis represents the amount of habitat (km) blocked by each structure, discounted by 50% for first-order streams, and by passability (25%, 50%, 0or 75%) of partial barriers. Barriers that were rehabilitated through implementation of this plan are shown, but other excluded structures (e.g., those found to be passable) are not.](content/images/hac-lnic.png){fig-align="left"}

## Map {.unnumbered} 

![Map of [focal species] habitat and structures that are confirmed or potential barriers to fish passage in the [X Watershed or X, Y, and Z watersheds] as of [dd month year]. Structure data were obtained from the Canadian Aquatic Barriers Database (aquaticbarriers.ca). The accessibility model represents areas of the watershed that [focal species] could access naturally in the absence of anthropogenic barriers. The habitat model represents the subset of accessible areas that may be used by [focal species for key habitat function(s)]. Available local knowledge and data were incorporated and overruled habitat model results. Thick light-blue lines represent habitat that is currently connected, whereas thick red lines represent habitat considered to be disconnected because it is upstream of barriers or unassessed structures. Barriers that were rehabilitated through implementation of this plan are shown, but other excluded structures (e.g., those found to be passable) are not.](content/images/map-cheti.png)

## Progress Summary {.unnumbered}
The planning process for the Cheticamp River Connectivity Rehabilitation Plan began in 2025. Since that time, # structures have been assessed to determine their fish passage status, and further assessments with habitat confirmations are planned for the 2026 field season. 
## Recommended Citation {.unnumbered}
